content.text: The file search tool provides hosted retrieval-augmented generation (RAG): upload documents to a vector store, then reference that store in your API calls. The model searches the store automatically, retrieves relevant chunks, and cites them in the answer — all server-side. You write no retrieval logic, no embedding code, and no chunking pipeline.

attrs.alt: File search architecture diagram showing upload, vector store, and query phases
attrs.title: File Search Architecture

content.text: The hosted file search tool is an OpenAI API feature (
). Anthropic does not currently offer a hosted vector store — for Claude, pass documents in message content blocks or build custom RAG with a vector DB.
Setting Up: Upload Files and Create a Vector Store
Preparation is a one-time step: upload your files, create a vector store, and attach the files to it. OpenAI handles chunking, embedding, and indexing automatically.

from openai import OpenAI
import pathlib, time

client = OpenAI()   # uses OPENAI_API_KEY from environment

# 1. Upload the document files
file_paths = ["handbook.pdf", "faq.txt", "policies.docx"]
uploaded_ids = []

for path in file_paths:
    p = pathlib.Path(path)
    if not p.exists():
        # Create a synthetic file for this example
        p.write_text(f"Sample content for {path}")
    with open(path, "rb") as fh:
        response = client.files.create(file=fh, purpose="assistants")
        uploaded_ids.append(response.id)
        print(f"Uploaded {path} -> {response.id}")

# 2. Create a vector store
vector_store = client.beta.vector_stores.create(
    name="Company Knowledge Base"
)
print(f"Vector store: {vector_store.id}")

# 3. Add files to the vector store and wait for indexing
batch = client.beta.vector_stores.file_batches.create_and_poll(
    vector_store_id=vector_store.id,
    file_ids=uploaded_ids
)
print(f"Indexing status: {batch.status}")
print(f"Files indexed: {batch.file_counts.completed} / {batch.file_counts.total}")
Pass the vector store ID in the
tool definition. OpenAI retrieves the most relevant chunks and injects them into the model's context before generating the response.
The model inserts citation markers like
in its response text when it draws from uploaded files. The
list on each text block maps each marker to a filename and quote.
Using the Chat Completions API with Assistants
If you prefer the older Assistants API, create an assistant with
enabled and attach the vector store to it. This pattern is useful for persisting conversation threads.

from openai import OpenAI
import time

client = OpenAI()
vector_store_id = "vs_abc123"

# Create an assistant with file_search enabled
assistant = client.beta.assistants.create(
    name="Knowledge Base Assistant",
    instructions="Answer questions based on the provided documents. Always cite your sources.",
    model="gpt-4o",
    tools=[{"type": "file_search"}],
    tool_resources={"file_search": {"vector_store_ids": [vector_store_id]}}
)

# Create a thread and run a question
thread = client.beta.threads.create()
client.beta.threads.messages.create(
    thread_id=thread.id,
    role="user",
    content="What are the key points of the employee handbook?"
)

run = client.beta.threads.runs.create_and_poll(
    thread_id=thread.id,
    assistant_id=assistant.id
)

if run.status == "completed":
    messages = client.beta.threads.messages.list(thread_id=thread.id)
    for msg in messages.data:
        if msg.role == "assistant":
            for block in msg.content:
                if block.type == "text":
                    print(block.text.value)
            break
Vector stores persist until deleted. List, update, and clean them up to avoid accumulating stale indexes that incur storage charges.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

# List all vector stores
stores = client.beta.vector_stores.list()
for vs in stores.data:
    print(f"{vs.id}: {vs.name} — {vs.file_counts.total} files — status: {vs.status}")

# Check files in a specific store
vs_id = "vs_abc123"
files = client.beta.vector_stores.files.list(vector_store_id=vs_id)
for f in files.data:
    print(f"  {f.id}: status={f.status}")

# Delete a store when no longer needed
# client.beta.vector_stores.delete(vs_id)
Building Custom File Search for Claude
Anthropic does not provide a hosted vector store. For Claude-based RAG, embed documents with any embedding model, store vectors in a vector DB, and expose search as a custom tool that Claude calls.

Attach metadata to uploaded files at creation time, then filter vector store searches to retrieve only documents matching specific attributes — department, date range, document type. This scopes retrieval for multi-tenant or domain-specific use cases.
in the tool definition to balance recall against precision. Raising the threshold reduces hallucination from low-quality matches; lowering it improves coverage for sparse corpora.
Combine file search with custom tools in a single request. The model uses file search to find context from documents and your custom tools for live data — the two work in parallel, not in sequence.
Comparing Hosted File Search vs Custom RAG
Choose the right approach for your scale and control requirements:
— upload files, create a store, reference it with
Retrieval is entirely server-side; no embedding or chunking code required on your end.
Anthropic (Claude) has no hosted vector store — pass small corpora as document blocks or build custom RAG with a vector DB and a
Delete unused vector stores to avoid ongoing storage charges.
For cross-model portability, a custom RAG tool works identically with OpenAI, Anthropic, and open models.
